publication: The Open Sourcing of Clark & Byrnes

items.title: We Bid You Anew
items.summary: Version 2 of Argon Bidding has Launched!
items.author: Blake Byrnes
items.contentHtml:
Argon has launched a new bidding mechanism that fixes several economic issues identified in the first epoch. Bidding is now done with regular argons. These Argons are used to guarantee liquidity to newly locked Bitcoins, and Vaults are able to earn a pro-rata share of these funds as the system is able to “mint” the new Argon demand.

Brief Backstory

As a reminder, we wrapped up the first phase (we’ll call this version 1) of bidding for our Proof of Authorized Work mining with several noted realizations. Among the biggest were that:

  1. Bidding for Mining Seats with a scarce asset (Bonded Argons) created a desire for unhealthy private vaults and private liquidity. In version 1 of bidding, a miner rented Bonded Argons from a Vault to bid on a mining seat. This was the ONLY way to beat other miners and take their spot for the next “cohort” of 10 miners. Bonded Argons could only be created by Vaults at a ratio equal to their locked Bitcoins. The way this played out was that every vault owner ended up procuring Bonded Argons intended to only be used for their own mining bids. The community comments on Discord revealed people felt other users using “their” vaults were “hacking” and “stealing liquidity”. The original purpose of Vaults was to make Bonded Argons affordable to miners. Creating private vaults would mean Bonded Argons remain with the vault operator, and we had a hard time imagining this system ever expanding into a robust functioning market.

  2. Vaults were too expensive, with too much friction. When a user set up a Vault in version 1, they allocated argons to 3 buckets: Bonded Argons, Bitcoin Locks and extra Bitcoin securitization. Every Bonded Argon cost you at least 2 argons (eg, 1 Bitcoin Argon = 1 Bonded Argons) and could only be activated when Bitcoins were locked into your vault. So you also needed to acquire or lock Bitcoins. This created too much friction for anyone simply trying to mine. We were eager to simplify vaults if possible.

  3. Mining bids were “rented” from a vault, which meant the actual cost to a miner could be drastically different than the amount of Argons put up. A Vault could offer 50 Bonded Argons, but charge a fee of 300 argons to bid with it (or any fee). The limited supply meant these were in high demand, but could only be sold to the first buyer. The lack of a liquid market made this a do-or-die race to be the first to acquire Bonded Argons. This meant new mining prospects could come armed with more argons than anyone else but couldn’t find Bonded Argons to bid with. We knew we needed to make this more of a market - either by making Bonded Argons a fungible asset, or by bidding with a free market asset.

  4. The technical setup for mining more than one seat was complicated. You needed a different wallet for each account, and you had to fund each account with both Argons and Argonots to make a bid. This was quite a lot of steps to do manually, requiring scripts and code just to bid on a few seats. We knew we wanted this to be simpler, but even the code was complicated.

The New System

In the new system, we’ve drastically simplified mining bids.

Mining Bids use Plain Argons

Bidding no longer requires a specialized asset. You can use any Argons (whether mined or acquired on an exchange) to bid whatever value you believe will yield a return. The top 10 bids are accepted at the end of the bidding period with these winning bids going into a Liquidity Pool (described below), and all losing bids returned. The only scarcity is the Argon circulation, which is built to expand to accommodate demand via its “minting” algorithms (see whitepaper 2 for detail). Mining bidders should always be able to acquire the assets they need to join the system.

Simplified, Non-Private Vaults

Vault creation now only requires a few choices related to your securitization ratio and fee structure. There is no longer a desire (or need) to think of your vault and your Bitcoins as providing liquidity for your own Mining Bids. Vaults can concentrate on serving Bitcoin holders. The loss in revenue from Bonded Argons is replaced by being able to earn a prorata share of the Mining Bids (using Liquidity Pools as described next).

Liquidity Pools

The winning bids for each cohort now goes into a “liquidity pool”. Vaults are able to earn a portion of this pool by locking up Argons for the duration of a cohort’s mining era (eg, 10 days). The liquidity pool offers the network 2 significant gains.

  1. 20% is burned, and will eventually fund a network treasury

  2. The liquidity will be used as a funding source for Bitcoins to continue moving into the network, even if the Argon price is depressed. Previously, you had to wait for the system to mint your argons when the price of argons rose to the target price. Without a guarantee this would ever occur, it was safer to wait before locking new Bitcoins. This was the single black swan risk of Argon’s Stabilization Mechanism (as described in Whitepaper #2). Liquidity pools allow us to eliminate this black swan event and encourage Bitcoins to come back into the system even after a mass sell-off.

To alleviate the financial commitment for Vaults, we’ve enabled vaults to solicit contributed funds from any Argon account (up to their amount of Locked Bitcoins). After each cohort bidding, the liquidity pool is distributed to vaults based on their pro-rata share of the total contributed capital. The vault can set the sharing percent they then pass on to contributors, and it automatically distributes these based on the pre-set deal.

Simpler Bidding

You can now bid for multiple seats from the same funding account, so you don’t have to setup and fund a wallet per-seat. You can also now share the same “signing keys” that need to be placed on a server, reducing the steps needed to bid on multiple mining seats.

New System Benefits

We believe this new system will:

  • Provide true market pricing to bidding - Argons and Argonots can be acquired through normal markets and the value of a mining slot can be calculated in order to make a “smart” bid. Argons are spent rather than held, so it’s a direct value exchange.

  • Make it cheaper and simpler to setup a vault - Vault operators are now only concerned with their bitcoin fees and how much they wish to offer in securitization collateral.

  • Keep a strong incentive to lock bitcoins - to earn a portion of the liquidity pools, a vault can raise capital up to the value of the bitcoins locked inside. This competition to earn a share will continue to incentivize bitcoins to move in. These Bitcoin produce a key stabilizer to avert Argon death spirals - priming an “Argon-short” that can remove excess liquidity any time demand for Argons is low (see whitepaper #2).

  • Remove the desire for private vaults - because “Bonded Argons” are no longer needed for Mining Bids, the only reason for wanting a private vault would be to lock your own Bitcoins. Although this desire might remain, far greater revenue can be earned by adding securitization to allow other Bitcoins to move in, which in turn can allow the capture of the liquidity pool profits.

How do I use the system?

The new bidding system launched on May 12th. You can participate in bidding using the Polkadot.js app or using typescript through our @argonprotocol/mainchain library or cli.

Below, you can find some example commands on how to create a bidding bot.

NOTE: you’ll first want to read up on how to acquire argons and set-up a mining machine.

You likely want to create a new wallet in Polkadot.js or a wallet like Talisman or the Polkadot Extension, fund it, and then export it to a file. This can help you reduce the exposure of your wallet funds for this account that you’ll wire up to your bidding bot.

  1. Create a .env file using your downloaded wallet json file

npx @argonprotocol/mainchain accounts create \
	--path=.env.bob --account-file-path=./bob.json \
	--account-passphrase=password1 \ 
        --register-keys=http://localhost:9944

  1. Start an automated bidder for the next cohort. It will run based on your parameters to try to maximize your mining seats.

npx @argonprotocol/mainchain --env=accounts/.env.bob \
	mining bid --min-bid=1 --max-bid=10 --bid-increment=0.1

If You Want Easy

We’re close to releasing an Argon Commander desktop app that will help anyone become a miner (no command lines or code needed). This app will automate the process of spinning up a cloud-server, calculate the ideal amounts to bid on mining seats, and start up a bot to win seats.

We’re starting a private beta of this app next week. If you’re interested in joining, reach out to @calebjclark on Discord.

The official release of Argon Commander is currently slated for June 2nd.

We’re excited for this next chapter. Hope you’ll join us.

Blake and Caleb

items.title: Argon Learnings from Epoch One
items.summary: Argon's first mining epoch (ten days of mining seats) ended on March 6th, 2025. This article dives into what went right, what went wrong, what we learned, and where we're headed.
items.author: Caleb Clark
items.contentHtml:
What an epoch!

In the Argon universe, epochs are ten-day mining cycles. Argon’s first epoch started on Monday, February 24th, 2025, at 17:00 GMT. Ten miners won the right to start mining through a special auction process. Over the next ten days, the remaining mining seats (10 per day, 100 total) were also auctioned off.

On Thursday, March 6th at 17:00 GMT, the first epoch finished. The original ten mining seats from February 24th were reassigned to new miners, and a new epoch began.

Here are a few quick highlights:

  • A total of 447 bids were placed in the first ten days. These bids were chasing 100 mining seats, which put the bid-to-seat ratio at 4 to 1. This surprised us. It was double what we were expecting.

  • The first ten seats won at a cumulative cost of 50 ARGNs. This was for all 10 seats (5 ARGNs per seat). Bidding reached a per-day high of nearly 500 ARGNs on February 27th; the lowest was 4.9 ARGNs on February 25th.

  • The market price of ARGN climbed to over $3 on Uniswap before Epoch One opened. As a result, Argon’s stabilization mechanism minted 12,316 argons during the first 24 hours to bring the price down.

  • Over $7k worth of Bitcoin (8,342,032 satoshis) was locked across nine Stabilization Vaults. This is substantial. It means that if Argon drops into a Terra-like death spiral tomorrow, there is already enough vaulted Bitcoins to restabilize an Argon circulation of ₳1.9M. Given that Argon’s current circulation is less than ₳35k (not ₳1.9M), Argon is overcollateralized by a factor of more than 50x. We suggest reading our 2nd whitepaper to understand the implications of this.

  • Much emotion was expressed on Argon’s Discord channels by those who lost the first round of bidding. The resulting dialog unearthed many insights for how we can improve our protocol.

Two things quickly stand out in the data:

  1. Every bidder on day one played it way too safe. The correct strategy would have been to go all-in. It’s easy to see this in hindsight. Day one was critical because in the days leading up to Epoch One, ARGN’s public price on Uniswap was well above its target price. According to Argon’s Stabilization Protocol, this meant a number of tokens — it ended up being ₳12,316 — would mint on day one, and all these tokens would accrue to the first ten mining seats. This meant the cost to earn ₳12,316 was only 50 ARGNs. It was a steal. Surprisingly, the smallest bidder at the table was sitting on 66.61 argons, which means any single player could have taken all ten seats and therefore collected this entire minting reward.

  2. Argon’s current auction process needs a major overhaul. The combination of how vaults function as public entities and the way Bonded Argons are locked into bids created a huge problem during Epoch One. What should have been simple auction economics turned into a complicated game of strategy and speed. Argon had a liquidity crisis. Although the system was designed to be open, the combination of these two features incentivized the network to privatize vaults and attempt to force competitors out. More on this below.

Some Details Worth Noting

  • One of our members (@dd204) uncovered a bidding bug on the 2nd day. The bug allowed owners of active bids to downsize their bids. This inadvertently allowed bidders to create havoc by submitting large bids that knocked other bidders out of the top ten. They could then downsize their large bids with cheaper winning bids in order to win at a much cheaper cost. Thankfully, none of this happened. We supplied a fix within 24 hours, before it had time to affect the outcome of any auctions.

  • Argon’s Stabilization Vaults were designed to operate as public entities. Anyone is allowed to set up vaults, and anyone is allowed to use vaults. Each vault publishes the fees they charge for their services — locking bitcoins and bonding argons. Anyone who uses a vault must pay these fees. In theory, vault owners should want lots of people using their vaults. However, the competitive nature of Epoch One drove a liquidity crisis that incentivized vault owners to try and create “private vaults”. By keeping others out, they could presumably gain the upper hand. This tension spilled over into many frustrated messages on the public Discord channels.

  • Closely related to the “private vault” situation was the rule whereby bitcoins are given a full ten days to finalize their move into a vault. The vault’s securitization funds are held in escrow until this finalization is complete. One such transaction took several days, which understandably irritated the vault owner because it temporarily limited their personal liquidity. As a result, we’ve decided to reduce the time required for finalization from ten days down to one.

  • At least one vault owner reported that programmatic scripts were used to lock bitcoins, borrow BAs, and place bids. A robust discussion took place on Discord with strong views on both sides of the spectrum. It is my opinion that scripting is a good thing. Over time, it should help improve the network's efficiency and liquidity. Regardless of one's opinion on the matter, I see no way to stop people from using scripts — it is one of the defining features of “programmatic money”.

  • Lastly, we arrive at the 12,316 argons that were minted during the first 24 hours. Miners who lost on the first day of bidding have expressed strong opinions. Some thought it unfair that those who bid a mere ₳50 earned so much upside, and they proposed rolling back the blockchain using a hard fork. They wanted to give themselves another chance at day one bids. While I can understand their morning-after regret, it is my opinion that the minting of these argons occurred completely above board for several reasons... A) the Argon minting protocol and the Uniswap price was publicly available for everyone to see, B) everyone had an equal ability to place bids based on this public knowledge, C) all the rules of the protocol were followed exactly as publicly stated, and D) the amount of minting on day one was minuscule compared to the quantity that will be needed over the coming days/weeks (i.e., there is no use quibbling over small peanuts when a much larger future awaits). In short, those who won the seats and minted the tokens earned them fairly.

Our Four-Pronged Solution

This "experimental" launch of Argon has been a huge win so far. It has given us the space to learn, iterate, and improve.

We’re moving forward with four main goals:

1) Fix the Obvious (TODAY)

We are in the process of pushing a new release. It includes several obvious improvements that emerged from recent community discussions:

  • IMPROVEMENT. Argon’s minting algorithm now uses TWAL (time weighted average liquidity) from the Uniswap pool to scale circulation increases that better match market dynamics.

  • BUG FIX. Bidders can no longer reduce bid amounts once submitted.

  • IMPROVEMENT. No more "bumping someone off the list" so that their vault funds become available. Failed bids are returned at the start of the next slot.

  • IMPROVEMENT. The window to finalize locking a bitcoin has been reduced from ten days down to one.

2) Create a Decentralized Treasury

Incorporating TWAL into Argon’s minting algorithm only partially resolves the minting situation that occurred on day one. Yes, it improves future minting, but it does not change the 12,316 argons that were already minted. However, the bigger issue is that Uniswap needs a much larger Argon liquidity pool. The larger the liquidity pool, the smaller the price swings.

The challenge of a fully decentralized rollout, such as we attempted with Argon, is that no entity was awarded tokens in the genesis block. Therefore, the network has no lead entity responsible for seeding the liquidity pools (or even the tokens if they wanted to do so).

Blake and I have spoken to several Epoch One miners about this need for increased liquidity, and most agree. A collective effort is gathering steam to create a Decentralized Argon Treasury. It looks as if at least 10,000 of the 12,316 previously minted argons will be donated to this Treasury.

Argon’s new treasury will be used to fund two things. First, it will dramatically increase Uniswap’s liquidity pool. Second, it will fund several marketing initiatives (more on this in a future post). Both initiatives will be a huge step forward for the Argon ecosystem. Please message me on Discord (@calebjclark) if you are interested in contributing.

3) Completely Revamp the Auction Process (NEXT WEEK)

Most of the community feedback expressed during Epoch One is legitimate, especially those related to bidding. The system inadvertently encouraged inefficient "racing" which injected unfortunate friction into the system.

We are in the final stage of a new release that will convert the Argon auction process into something much more akin to how real-world live auctions work. There will be no locking up of Bonded Argons into bids. There will be no more racing to use vault space.

The best way to understand our new auction process is by reading the pull request on Github. Please leave relevant comments in the PR, and if you have proposed code changes, please submit a pull request of your own.

A dedicated blog post on this new auction process is hopefully coming next week.

4) Provide Easier Tooling (VERY SOON)

We want Argon to be usable by everyone, not just those who are technically inclined. Our first step in this journey is to make mining easy enough for our parents to use (hey, it’s good to have a goal, right?)

Blake and I are working on the final touches of a new desktop app called Argon Commander. It will allow you to set up and manage a full-blown mining machine with nothing but a laptop, a mouse, and a cheap cloud machine.

Below are a few screenshots. A dedicated blog post is on the way. Our goal is to have a rough beta of the desktop app ready to use by the end of March.

Next Steps

The best way to get up to speed is to read Argon’s source code. It includes all the rules and weights that determine the auction outcome.

Another great resource is our Discord channel, where you’ll find a community of other like-minded miners.

Our first two whitepapers have been published here and here.

Finally, you should subscribe to this Substack channel. It’s the best way to stay up-to-date on the latest Argon news:

Subscribe now

items.title: The Basics of Argon Bidding
items.summary: Argon's bootstrapping phase will soon end, and when it does, Argon's consensus mechanism will flip from PoCW to PoAW. This article provides high-level details for how Argon's mining seats work.
items.author: Caleb Clark
items.contentHtml:
WARNING: ARGON IS EXPERIMENTAL Argon Network is launching in an experimental state. We are releasing it with no guarantees and no expectations. It contains some fascinating and novel concepts around how to create a stable crypto asset, but it's not perfect. We hope curious individuals will help us test it and push it. We want to learn its weaknesses and fix its flaws.

We hoped to close out Argon’s bootstrapping phase ten days into the mainnet, but we were forced to delay the transition until next week due to unforeseen technical difficulties.

Regardless of the minor hiccup, the first ten days were beyond expectations.

Over one hundred mining nodes across twenty-nine mining groups (based on account IDs) participated in Argon’s bootstrapping. Although these mining nodes are anonymous — we can’t identify the individuals behind them — we were able to ascertain their geolocation distributions through IP address lookups. Activity was seen around the globe, from the western United States to the southern flatlands of Denmark, from the Cayman Islands to Belgium, from Singapore to Brazil.

Over the next several days, trading pools will open on Uniswap for both Argon and Argonot tokens. This trading will mark the beginning of the transition from Proof-of-Compute-Work (PoCW) to Proof-of-Authorized-Work (PoAW). Once PoAW becomes the network’s de facto consensus mechanism, only those with mining seats will be able to create blocks and earn rewards.

This article explores how mining seats work, the various rules that drive mining economics, and most importantly, how to win a seat.

Understanding Proof-of-Authorized-Work

Proof-of-Authorized-Work (PoAW) is our novel consensus mechanism for creating an efficient proof-of-work blockchain. Some might describe it as a blend between proof-of-work and proof-of-stake, but this is too simplistic of a view. It’s better classified as a new category of consensus. PoAW randomly distributes block creation to a preselected list of miners through the use of payment activity representing real-world economic activity.

The following diagram shows the high-level flow of PoAW, beginning with payments being transacted and ending with rewards being mined on the chain:

Taxation is the fundamental foundation of PoAW. It’s 20% or ₳0.20 per P2P transaction on the localchain, whichever fee is less. Taxes on the Argon network behave very differently from those of centralized governments. Because Argon is decentralized, there are no trusted entities to enforce taxation. Instead, each user levies their own taxes through protocol-level enforcement.

Argon taxes serve a unique role in the process of choosing which miner closes a block, and as recognition for this role, the taxpayer is awarded 25% of the block rewards. This inadvertently flips the traditional view of taxation from a cost-of-doing business into a benefit-of-doing business. Ultimately, these taxes subsidize payment costs across the network.

Our soon-to-be-released 3rd whitepaper dives into the details of PoAW economics and its ability to lower the settlement cost of peer-to-peer payments. In the meantime, we encourage you to explore Argon’s codebase, which is open-source and available on Github.

Staggered Mining Seats

In PoCW — as with most other forms of proof-of-work — there is no defined list of authorized miners. Anyone can join, and anyone can create blocks so long as they show proof of solving the publicly stated cryptographic puzzle.

PoAW is different from PoCW in that it requires participating miners to be on an authorized list. This list is free (as in free speech) for anyone to join, however, the list has a finite number of seats. Participants are determined through a time-constrained auctioning process.

The network’s bidding will begin with 100 seats, although we expect this to increase to 10,000 seats as the network grows. The winner of a seat holds it for a full “epoch”. In the Argon Network, an “epoch” is ten days, or to be more accurate, 14,400 ticks (ticks are minutes). Bidding opens 24 hours (1,400 ticks) before the seat is allowed to start mining.

Seats reopen for bidding every ten days (or 14,400 ticks).

Although there are 100 seats within each epoch, they do not all open for bidding at the same time. Instead, the network staggers bidding across the ten days: ten seats per day. See the following illustration.

Two tokens are used in the bidding process:

  1. Argonots. These are Argon Ownership Tokens, and they’re used to ensure miners have a vested interest in the long-term stability of the network. A pre-set number of tokens must be attached to a bid for it to be valid.

  2. Bonded Argons. These are standard argons that have been uniquely bonded to a Stabilization Vault. More on this below.

The latter bullet introduced two concepts (Bonded Argons + Stabilization Vaults). Both deserve more detailed explanations.

Bonded Argons

Bonded Argons (BAs) might appear to be an unnecessary complication of the system, but on deeper analysis, you’ll find they are one of our most fundamental innovations. They connect Bitcoin’s store-of-value property with Argon’s mining operations to create a self-reinforcing stabilization and growth mechanism.

The most important thing to understand about Bonded Argons is this:

Bonded Argons are the key to procuring mining seats.

Bonded Argons are the determining factor in who wins seats. Mining bids with the greatest number of Bonded Argons win, and the only way to get Bonded Argons is by borrowing them from a Stabilization Vault.

One of Bonded Argon’s biggest innovations is its lending protection mechanism. Vaults create BAs by wrapping argons (it’s a one-to-one ratio) into a specialized loan instrument. These BAs can be lent to aspiring miners with no risk of loan defaults — Bonded Argons automatically revert to the lender whenever the mining seat finishes or if the miner loses their bid for the seat. The lender can then turn around and lend them to someone else.

It is cryptographically impossible for the borrower to default on their loan or stop BAa from reverting back to the Vault at the end of their loan. While a seemingly small technical feature, this fundamentally changes the financial mechanics of lending. In traditional finance, the borrower’s cost (and therefore the expected lender’s returns) must incorporate the risk of loan defaults within their portfolios.

Stabilization Vaults

Stabilization Vaults are the only entities allowed to create Bonded Argons (BAs). BAs are generated through the process of liquid locking a bitcoin. You can read more about liquid locking here and here.

For the purpose of this article, it is important to note that Vaults must adhere to the following rules:

  • Vaults must stake argons equal to the market value of the bitcoins they would like to liquid lock (Locked Bitcoins). These staked argons are used as securitization capital that will be forfeited if the Vault colludes in moving the bitcoin against protocol rules. This securitization is held for a full year.

  • Locked Bitcoins cannot mint more than 50% of the total argons in circulation. This means if Locked Bitcoins have already minted 50% of circulation, and a new bitcoin moves into a vault, that new bitcoin is placed into a FIFO queue to await its minting turn.

  • Once bitcoins are locked into a Vault, the receiving Vault is allowed to create Bonded Argons at an amount equal to or less than the market value of the bitcoins in their Vault (as calculated at the moment of locking).

The actual work performed by Vaults is minimal. They’re basically just storing half a multisig. However, their capital requirements are substantial, as outlined above, and in return, Vaults have several monetization options:

  • Bitcoin Locking Fees. Vaults are required to lock up argons equal to the market value of incoming bitcoin, and Vaults deserve to earn a yield on their capital. We expect this fee to settle into a 4-5% range over time (this is a small cost to pay for the incredible benefits of liquid locking). Since Argon is inflation-resistant, this 4-5% is layered on top of the inflation, which means, that if inflation is running at 3%, the Vault’s effective yield is closer to 7-8%.

  • Bonded Argon Lending Fees. Every Bonded Argon wraps a regular argon, and Vaults can earn an additional yield on this wrapped capital. We expect this fee to float between 20-30% (annualized). It is implemented as a loan fee paid by the miners who are borrowing the Bonded Argons.

Although the monetization yields mentioned above might seem low for defi standards, the inability for Bonded Argons to default on their loans is quite novel. We will be interested to see how this plays out over time in the financial markets.

The following illustration shows the interconnection between Bitcoins, Argons, Stabilization Vaults, and Bonded Argons.

The monetization and business model of Stabilization Vaults is a fascinating topic, but we’ll save their details for future writings. The important thing in this article is how Bonded Argons are used to win mining seats.

For the technical details on setting up and running a vault, see our Getting Started guide on How To Run a Vault.

The Bidding Process

The most basic requirement for a valid mining bid is that it must “stake” argonots. I’m using the word “stake” loosely since Argon’s concept of staking is quite different from Ethereum and other proof-of-stake networks. The similarities are that the argonots are “locked” for the duration of the mining seat, and if you break the rules your “stake” might be eaten. However, at the moment there are no stake-eating mechanisms enabled.

The first round of bidding will begin with “stake” set to 0.5 argonots. Everyone is required to stake the same amount. Attaching more arognots will not help your bid. The quantity will adjust over time to encourage 120% of the number of bids as there are seats available.

As the following illustration shows, incoming bids must be higher than the lowest active bid or they will be rejected.

Similarly, incoming bids that are higher than the lowest bid push any lower bids down the stack, which bumps the lowest bid out of the top-ten stack.

Bids that are lost are immediately disassembled. This is true regardless of whether it was an existing bid that lost to an incoming bid or whether it was an incoming bid that failed to outbid existing bids. Whatever the reason, Bonded Argons from losing bids immediately revert back to the Vaults they were borrowed from. The bidder can then reborrow their newly released BAs to assemble another bid.

Except for Epoch One, which closes exactly 1,440 ticks after opening, the auction closing time for all other epochs is randomly chosen. This makes the auction ending time unpredictable, which reduces the risk of auction sniping.

Mining Rewards

Miners who win seats are allowed to participate in block creation for the duration of their ten-day window. Unlike traditional proof-of-work algorithms, Argon’s PoAW uses a commit-reveal process to ensure near-uniform block distribution across active mining seats. This means if there are 100 miners in anx epoch, and you win one of the seats, you should expect to win an average of 1% of the blocks (144 blocks).

Each block is awarded a combination of three things:

Argons. The first post-genesis block in the chain was awarded 0.5 argons. Rewards increase by 0.01 argons every 118 ticks until it peaks at 5.0 argons. Seventy-five percent is awarded to the miners who create the blocks; the remaining twenty-five percent is awarded to the tax authorities who originate the block votes.

Argonots. These begin the same as argon rewards. The first post-genesis block granted 0.5 argonots. It increases by 0.001 every 118 ticks until it reaches 5.0 argonots. From that point forward it will follow the halving formula of bitcoin. Seventy-five percent is awarded to miners who create the blocks; the remaining twenty-five percent is awarded to tax authorities who originate the block votes.

Transaction Fees. 100% of the transaction fees within a block are awarded to the miner who creates the block.

In addition to the rewards listed above, active miners also receive a pro-rata distribution of new argons that are minted by the system whenever Argon’s price rises above target. This could result in zero or more rewards.

Next Steps

The best way to get up to speed before bidding starts is to study Argon’s source code. It includes all the rules and weights that determine the auction outcome.

You might also find value in our How to Run a Miner guide, especially in the How to Bid section.

Another great resource is our Discord channel where you’ll find a vibrant community of other miners.

Finally, you should subscribe to this Substack channel. It’s the best way to stay up-to-date on the latest Argon news:

Subscribe now

items.title: Argon Is Live On the Mainnet
items.summary: Although we're upgrading Argon from the Testnet to the Mainnet, unlike most other crypto projects, we hope everyone will continue to treat Argon as an experiment for at least another year.
items.author: Caleb Clark
items.contentHtml:
Earlier this evening, the last pre-launch commit was pushed into Argon’s code repository. A few minutes ago, the first Mainnet block was mined from a small strip of land in the Caribbean Sea.

It’s an exciting milestone. Blake and I have worked for nearly six years to bring this project to life. However, today isn’t our destination; it’s merely our beginning.

The current state of Argon can be best summed up with this phrase:

A Grand Experiment

What do we mean by an “experiment”? Is the Mainnet just another Testnet? Do we expect tokens on the Mainchain be succeeded by something else?

First off, no, Argon Mainnet is NOT a temporary testnet. We designed it to last for the long haul. Argons and argonots that were mined earlier today will likely be trading a thousand years from now.

By “experiment” we mean, let’s take hype off the table. Let’s remove the desire for short-term financial gain. Let’s spend the time we need to build a world-class currency the right way.

In order to advance our long-term objectives, we made two critical decisions that are rare in the crypto space. First, we launched Argon with no pre-mining, no grants in the genesis block, and no rewards of any kind to the founders or early developers. Second, we reduced block rewards during its first year of launch by over 90%.

Instead of incentivizing influencers to pump and dump argonots (Argon Ownership Tokens) on the crypto market, we’d rather begin with a small group of people who believe in our mission. We want people who see the long-term value of a stable crypto asset, people who realize the world needs a currency that is protected from inflation, immune to death spirals, and fully decentralized.

Instead of incentivizing thousands of bitcoins to quickly move into Argon’s Stabilization Vaults, we’d rather start with one bitcoin. There is great value in listening to feedback, experimenting with ideas, and iterating on the product.

Argon doesn’t need to beat out Tether, USDC, or Ethena on day one. We’d rather start slow, work out the kinks, and win in the end.

Don’t misunderstand us. We’re ambitious. We designed Argon with the capability of growing into a global medium of exchange, but we also designed it to remain stable without needing an ever-increasing cycle of hype and momentum.

Unlike most other stablecoins, Argon works quite well in the micro. Only a few argons need minting and only a partial bitcoin needs vaulting for the system to fully sustain itself.

And so…

Let’s spend the next few months playing with the Argon, testing its edges, and hardening its security. If we focus on giving the world something of lasting value, growth will surely come.

How to Get Involved

There are many ways you can join our movement. Here are two links to help you get started:

→ Introduction to Argon Mining

→ Accessing the Mainnet

Reach out to us on Discord if you have any questions, feedback, or ideas for improvement.

Thanks for reading The Open Sourcing of Clark & Byrnes! Subscribe to receive updates.

items.title: An Introduction to Argon Mining
items.summary: How does Argon mining work? How long will the compute phase last? When does the bidding phase begin? What type of rewards can I earn? Will there be pre-mining?
items.author: Caleb Clark
items.contentHtml:
Argon launches on the Mainnet in four days. January 15, 2025!

WARNING: ARGON IS EXPERIMENTAL
Argon Network is launching in an experimental state. We are releasing it with no guarantees and no expectations. It contains some fascinating and novel concepts around how to create a stable crypto asset, but it's not perfect. We hope curious individuals will help us test it and push it. We want to learn its weaknesses and fix its flaws.

Blake and I have been inundated with mining questions since announcing Argon’s Mainnet launch. We’re actively working on our third whitepaper, which lays out the details of mining operations. In the meantime, this article (along with our codebase) must suffice.

TLDR

For those who want a quick summary:

  • Argon has no pre-mining, no ICOs, and no rewards granted in the genesis block. All participants start with an equal ability to mine and own the network.

  • Mining does not require any specialized hardware. The first ten days of the network uses a compute algorithm that is ASIC resistant. The network then switches to authorized mining seats, which are both CPU and memory light.

  • Block rewards distribute two tokens: argons (stablecoins) and argonots (ownership tokens). They begin at 0.5 tokens of each per block. They increase by 0.001 every 118 blocks until peaking at 5 per block, at which point the argonot follows Bitcoin’s halving algorithm.

The first three sections lay out Argon’s basic fundamentals. The last three describe specific mining rewards and various steps for getting involved.

1. Three Tokens

Argon Network incorporates three types of tokens (two of which are native):

Argons - These are designed to be stable assets that are inflation resistant and immune to death spirals. The network adjusts the number of tokens in circulation based on the current supply and demand of the market. You can read more about Argon’s stabilization design in our second whitepaper. Argon is designed to start at a value of 1-to-1 with the dollar ($1.00 should buy ₳1.00). However Argon’s value is tied to an inflation index, which assuming the dollar continues its debasement trend, Argon’s value will rise in relative comparison.

Argonots - These Argon Ownership Tokens serve several valuable roles in the network. First, they serve as governance votes in the maintenance and management of the network. Second, they determine who can bid for mining seats during Proof-of-Authorized-Work — valid bids require a predetermined amount of argonots.

Locked Bitcoins - Yep, these are the same bitcoins everyone loves. They serve a twofold purpose in the Argon Network. Bitcoins participate in minting new argons whenever market demand increases. Second, Bitcoins serve as exogenous stabilization assets whenever Argon drops below its target price (more about this in our second whitepaper). In both cases, Bitcoins are given a prime role and profit opportunity in the Argon.

2. Hybrid Proof-of-Work

Argon’s blocks are organized into ten day slots. The first slot (Slot Zero) is different from all the slots that come after. It has a special bootstrapping role since no funds are granted in the genesis block. The network must emerge into existence without a centralized entity or pre-mined tokens guiding its path. It does this through sole reliance on our Proof-of-Compute-Work consensus.

Argon uses two different proof-of-work (PoW) consensus models:

Proof-of-Compute-Work (PoCW) is an ASIC-resistant proof-of-work design that uses the Randomx algorithm. Randomx was developed as part of the Monero blockchain to create a proof of work that does not favor GPUs, allowing regular desktops to compete. It is the sole proof-of-work algorithm used during "Slot Zero", and following the end of Slot Zero, PoCW becomes the fallback algorithm for potential edge cases that are possible in a Proof-of-Authorized-Work.

Proof-of-Authorized-Work (PoAW) distributes block closings to a predefined set of miners. It requires miners to bid for a limited set of seats in an open auction. Miners who win seats hold them for the slot duration, after which, the seats are reopened for auction. Instead of competing to close blocks based on compute power, PoAW awards block closings based on a commit reveal algorithm. This ensures near-uniform distribution across active mining seats. Miners who want to win seats must stake a predetermined amount of argonots for the slot’s duration. If there are more bids than open seats, bids are chosen based on the amount of Bonded Argons attached to the bid. Bonded Argons are argons that have been put through a special Bitcoin Bonding Processs (see the Stabilization Vaults section below).

During the first ten days of the network’s Slot Zero launch, PoCW is the only active consensus algorithm being used. On day eleven, which is the start of Slot One, PoAW activates and takes over as the dominant algorithm. Although PoCW is still allowed, blocks created by PoAW always have priority. Therefore, except in the extreme edge case where there are no registered or active mining seats (i.e., because of a catastrophic global doomsday scenario), PoAW will remain the only consensus algorithm of the network.

Blocks are generally mined every minute, although during PoCW, this timeframe could vary substantially.

3. Stabilization Vaults

Stabilization Vaults are designed to securely hold bitcoins within Argon’s Liquid Locking mechanism. There are many benefits to Liquid Locking for bitcoin holders, but we’ll save that for another article.

Anyone can setup and run vaults. As bitcoins enter vaults, they trigger a process whereby vault owners are allowed to convert regular argons into a special type of asset called Bonded Argons.

Bonded Argons are used to win mining seats. The more Bonded Argons attached to a bid, the higher their priority over other bids. Therefore, as Argon mining seats increase in value, the demand for Bonded Argons should also increase.

Bonded Argons contain a special lending feature whereby vaults can loan them to miners without risk of a loan default. Once the ten day slot finishes, Bonded Argons automatically revert back to their vault owner, which can subsequently be lent to another miner. It is cryptographically impossible for the borrowing miner to stop these Bonded Argons from reverting.

There is a rule that says the amount of Bonded Argons created by a vault cannot exceed the market value of bitcoins within the vault. So if $100k worth of bitcoins have moved into a vault, the vault owner is limited to creating no more than $100k worth of Bonded Argons.

We plan to write follow-up articles about Stabilization Vaults, Bitcoins, Bonded Argons, and their many interdependencies. For now, we invite you to dive into the codebase, read our whitepaper, and experiment with Liquid Locking and our Stabilization Analysis Model.

4. Block Rewards

As previously mentioned, blocks are generally mined every minute (this can shift during PoCW). Miners who create blocks earn mining rewards in the form of argons and argonots.

The following chart shows the mining rewards earned per block. It starts at 0.5 argons/argonots in the starting block and climbs to 5 argons/argonots by end of year one (an additional 1 milligon is added every 118 blocks). Starting at the beginning of year one, block rewards for argonots follow bitcoin’s halving formula. And both cap at 21,000,000 tokens.

Argon tokens are handled slightly differently. Just like argonots, argons begin at 0.5 tokens per block and climb to 5 by the end of year one. However, argons do not follow the halving formula. They remain at 5 tokens per block.

The primary difference between argonots versus bitcoins, as it relates to block rewards, is that Bitcoin’s blocks are mined on average once every 10 minutes while Argon’s blocks are mined once every minute. Therefore, since Bitcoin’s halving occurs every 210,000 blocks, Argon’s must occur every 2,100,000 (it’s the same time length, roughly every four years). Regardless, the number of tokens earned across the same timeframe is equal.

Chart: Mining Rewards Per Block

In addition to the 0.5-5 argons/argonots mined per block, miners can also earn argons through minting. Whenever the demand for Argon pushes the market price above its target price, the algorithm triggers argon minting. Newly minted argons are equally distributed among all active miners. Of course, if there is no demand increase during a slot then there are no minting rewards for miners. Only the basic block rewards.

You’re probably wondering why Argon’s block rewards begin at 0.5 argons instead of the full 5. The purpose is to give Argon a full year to grow into its shoes. By lowering the rewards by 90%, we hope to reduce the boom and bust hype-cycles prevelant in so many crypto coins. We want to give Argon the space to be experimental. We want to give the market time to play with it, test it, and improve it. It’s how so many of the great technologies started. Slowly at first.

5. Becoming a Miner During Slot Zero

Mining in Slot Zero is incredibly simple. All you need is a laptop and some know-how. Argon uses Monero’s ASIC-resistant PoW algorithm, which means any semi-powerful computer is a viable mining machine.

The beauty of Slot Zero is that everyone, including me and Blake, will be playing by the same rules. There will be no pre-mined tokens given to the founding developers. There will be no capital required to participate. Just download our mining software and run it. That’s it. As your machine mines blocks, you earn rewards.

During the 10 days of Slot Zero, miners will compete to create an estimated 14,400 blocks (1 block per minute, or per tick, as we call it). As detailed in the Block Rewards section above, each block awards 0.5 argons and 0.5 argonots per block, and it increases by 0.001 every 118 blocks.

The following formula calculates the argons and argonots earned during Slot Zero. An estimated 8,071 argons and another 8,071 argonots will be collected in aggregate across all miners. It is expected that Slot Zero miners will use these tokens to place mining bids for Slot One, or they will sell them on the market to those who do.


Our plan is to publish the final pre-launch code commit on January 15th. We will then bring the network into existence and publish the initial node IP addresses here on Substack and on our Discord channels.

Click here for instructions on setting up your miners and testing them against the Argon Testnet. It’s the best way to be ready for Mainnet.

Use the subscribe button below to ensure you’re notified the moment Argon’s Mainnet is ready for mining.

Subscribe now

6. Becoming a Miner After Slot Zero

Slot One is when the real network begins.

Instead of competing against an infinite number of other miners based solely on who throws the most compute power at the network, participating in Slot One is primarily based on the cost of submitting a winning bid.

Argon has a finite number of mining seats available in each 10 day slot. The network will start with 100 mining seats. We expect to increase this number to 10,000 in the future, although we have no definitive plans for when this increase will begin.

Slots are available for bidding on an evenly distributed rolling basis across each of the 10 days. This means that 10 slots will open for bidding on day one, another 10 on day two, and so on. This creates a staggered overlap between miners rolling in and out across the slots.

Going Deeper

Look for a follow-up article next week with specifics on setting up mining bids in Slot One and beyond.

In the meantime, dive into our codebase here. Or read our instructions for getting started. Also, our first two whitepapers have been published here and here.

Reach out to us on Discord if you have any questions or feedback.

items.title: The Future of Ulixee Is Argon
items.summary: On January 15, 2025, Ulixee will launch the Argon Protocol, a novel medium of exchange that is protected from inflation, immune to death spirals, and powered by Bitcoin.
items.author: Caleb Clark
items.contentHtml:
Blake and I have exciting news.

Within the next month, Ulixee will step into the payments space — or, to be more precise, into the world of currency.

Wait, what?!?!?

Yes, I know this news might surprise many of you — it’s been an unexpected journey for us too — but it aligns perfectly with Ulixee’s long-term roadmap.

Let me back up for a moment.

A Journey Into Reopening the Web

Blake and I started working full-time on Ulixee nearly six years ago. Our kickoff breakfast was June 12, 2018, at The Silver Skillet in Atlanta, Georgia.

We believed the internet's business model was fundamentally broken, and we wanted to help fix it.

When Tim Berners-Lee launched the web in 1989, it was meant to be an open, collaborative network. Its community-driven spirit was exemplified by the “View Source” option in every browser menu. The ethos of the early web was that data should be used, reused, linked and shared.

Today, although the “View Source” can still be found in your browser, the collaborative-spirit has largely disappeared. The internet is now dominated by advertising giants whose business models maximize viewership by stifling competitors and promoting walled gardens. The web has transformed from a collaborative space into a battleground of competitive moats.

In the early 2000s, a movement began to reopen the web, encouraging data mashups, interoperability, and semantic markup. Though it began with energy, it eventually fizzled due to conflicting technical, social, and economic struggles.

Our conversations at The Silver Skillet revolved around reigniting the web’s early spirit.

In today’s society, it is generally assumed that a website owns everything related to it — its brand, its layout, its data. But does it?

We began exploring an alternative, more legally grounded viewpoint, which is that most of the web’s data is neither ownable or copyrightable. These are simple facts — facts about flights, sports statistics, weather, real estate listings, tech stacks, local events, fun restaurants, product pricing, and job postings. As facts, they are owned by no one and yet owned by everyone. While a website’s branding and design might be copyrightable, its underlying raw data often is not.

However, just because most of the web’s data is legally free doesn’t mean it’s easy to access or use.

The Challenge of Accessing Data

Getting access to good data is difficult.

For example, a web developer with a cool app idea — say, combining Ticketmaster events, OpenTable reservations and flight schedules — must negotiate individual data deals with all 229 relevant entities (1 Ticketmaster + 1 OpenTable + 227 commercial airlines). The startup will likely die before negotiations are complete.

In many instances, scraping is the only real option, but its complicated, messy, and time consuming. Although the data you need is usually on the web and accessible to human users, there is no ready-to-use machine-readable layer of the web that software can tap into.

Scraping Is Easy to Start but Hard to Scale

The world is filled with scraping libraries that help load webpages and extract data. However, scaling these scripts for large datasets introduces edge cases that become seemingly infinite. Minor tag changes can break the logic, website redesigns require script rewrites, and issues like captchas, pop-ups, and bot blockers compound the challenge.

We built Double Agent to test how bot blockers could detect automated scraping, analyzing everything from DOM fingerprinting to TCP variations across OSes. We discovered hundreds of thousands of possible detections. This led to The Unblocked Project, an automated browser architecture that is resistent to bot blockers detection.

Next, we developed Hero, a simpler, more flexible tool for writing data extraction scripts. Hero uses Unblocked as its underlying browser engine, layering in a number of features such as the AwaitedDOM, Flow Handling, Replay, and Time Travel.

Finally, we tackled the inefficiency of duplicated scraping. The top 1,000 websites receive heavy scraper traffic, often from bots all extracting the same data. The lack of collaboration between these many bots create massive inefficiencies, not only for the bot developers but also for the websites that are being hammered.

Our Datastore protocol/library wraps existing scraper scripts into discrete composable units of data that function as “Lego-like” blocks. These data blocks can be merged, piped, stacked, and mashed without any need for the scripts to pre-collaborate or even explicitly agree to work together. It’s an anonymous network that turns every website into an API.

Our biggest Datastore innovation is their Layer1/Layer2 architecture. It allows multiple scraper scripts to share the same request/response rails. This decreases the cost for scrapers and reduces the server load for websites.

Ensuring Fair Compensation

Datastores will soon be able to handle transactional micropayments. Each datastore can set its own price per query, allowing developers to directly monetize their contributions. For example, the following datastore code sets a base price of 1/1000th of a penny:

new Datastore({
  name: 'HelloWorld',
  extractors: {
    basic: new Extractor({
      basePricePerQuery: 1, // in microgons (1/1000th of a penny)
      ...
    }),
  }
});

Additionally, Datastore cloning now cryptographically retains the ancestral payment hierarchy. This ensures that each cloned datastore fairly compensates upward in the stack, thereby rewarding all participants in the current data query.

Datastores are not limited to scraper scripts. They are designed to handle any dataset, including SQL databases, flat files, and more. Developers simply set a per-query fee and can instantly start generating revenue—no need for Stripe accounts, cross-border payment complications, or bank accounts.

Ungated Payments Are Paramount

One reason the web achieved such global success was its open, decentralized nature. It wasn’t controlled by a gatekeeper. There were no barriers to entry, no central authorities dictating participation. Anyone could join, innovate, and disrupt.

The same is needed for a globally spanning data layer.

It is essential for Ulixee to avoid centralized controls, especially within its payment mechanism. The hierarchical processes of traditional banking systems are inherently at odds with open decentralization. Visa, Stripe, and other payment networks have demonstrated both the ability and willingness to shut down activities they dislike.

An effective data layer requires three key components:

  1. Ungated Payment Settlement: The system must have alternatives to traditional banking to ensure transactions cannot be unilaterally blocked.

  2. Per-Query Payments: The system must allow users pay only for what they use (i.e. micropayments that operate on a per-query basis), which eliminates the need for bundled subscriptions, which eliminates the need for user accounts that can be unilaterally blocked.

  3. Seamless Cross-Border Transactions: Software development is global in nature, but especially when it comes to web scraping. Many of the companies using scraped data reside in countries that are different from where the scraper scripts are being written.

Crypto technologies provide solutions to these challenges but they also come with their own set of limitations.

A Stable Medium of Exchange

While crypto offers many strengths, one of its significant weaknesses has been price volatility. Stablecoins have attempted to address this issue, but most rely on centralized, off-chain assets for stability. Even decentralized stablecoins like Dai ultimately depend on assets tied to centralized systems.

For information on the issues of using centralized, off-chain assets for stability, see our first whitepaper, On the Stabilization of Collateral Backed Stablecoins.

Blake and I have been working on stablecoin designs since the fall of 2018. After exploring countless approaches, we continued to face the same core issue: the more decentralized the asset backing the stablecoin, the greater its risk of death-spiraling. Over-collateralization can reduce this risk, but it doesn’t eliminate it entirely. As they say, given enough time, every potential black swan event will occur.

By the spring of 2023, we had almost given up. Then we stumbled upon an idea so simple it seemed almost absurd. Instead of using decentralized assets to collateralize a stablecoin, we realized we could reverse the approach and use decentralized assets to derivatively short the stablecoin. This mechanism creates profit incentives on both sides of the target price, ensuring the faster it deviates, the faster it rebounds.

For a detailed explanation, see our second whitepaper, The Fundamentals for Creating a Stable Crypto Asset. We also invite you to explore our simulation engines: Liquid Locking for Bitcoin and our Stabilization Analysis Model.

Our Argon Launch Plan

Argon has been running in Testnet for several months, and we’re on track for a Mainnet launch by January 15, 2025.

In a major departure from most crypto projects, we have decided that Argon will launch with zero genesis block rewards and zero ICO pre-sales. That’s right — no tokens will be pre-allocated to founders, investors, or other stakeholders. Instead, everyone will be given an equal opportunity to participate in the network's growth and mining rewards.

In full transparency, Blake and I are in the process of establishing a company and raising a seed round to build ideas on top of Argon. We believe Argon’s ecosystem will create tremendous opportunities for a wide range of products and services, and we plan to be among the first to build within this ecosystem.

However, none of us, including our new company, past company, etc, will be given any pre-launch or post-launch favors. Our only value will come from what we earn alongside all of you once the network launches. All code that we have written is being fully released to the world under the MIT license. We are also releasing all our trademarks, pending patents, and other related intellectual property.

We recently set up an Argon Foundation to hold the project’s web domains, github accounts, and any other centralized assets that are needed for basic operations. However, aside from these small items, the foundation is expected to hold no other economic value.

Next Steps for the Community

We’d love to have you join our movement. Below are a few ways you can participate:

  • Read our whitepapers and join the Argon Discord channel.

  • View our Getting Started Guide and help us put Testnet through the paces.

  • Work with us on improving the code and preparing Argon for launch.

  • Consider a financial sponsorship through Github.
